title: Compiler Design — Phases of Compilation
examTip: 6 phases: Lexical Analysis → Syntax Analysis → Semantic Analysis → Intermediate Code Gen → Code Optimization → Code Generation. First 3 = Front End (analysis). Last 3 = Back End (synthesis). Symbol Table and Error Handler span all phases.

phases.name: 1. Lexical Analysis (Scanner)
phases.description: Reads source code → converts to Tokens (keywords, identifiers, operators, literals). Removes whitespace and comments.

phases.name: 2. Syntax Analysis (Parser)
phases.description: Checks grammatical structure using grammar rules (BNF/EBNF). Builds a Parse Tree (Concrete Syntax Tree) or AST.

phases.name: 3. Semantic Analysis
phases.description: Type checking, scope resolution, checking undeclared variables, type compatibility. Annotates AST with type information.

phases.name: 4. Intermediate Code Generation
phases.description: Generates machine-independent intermediate representation: Three-Address Code (TAC), Quadruples, or SSA form.

phases.name: 5. Code Optimization
phases.description: Improves IR for faster execution or smaller code: constant folding, dead code elimination, loop unrolling, common subexpression elimination.

phases.name: 6. Code Generation (Target Code)
phases.description: Translates optimized IR into target machine code or assembly. Handles register allocation, instruction selection, and instruction scheduling.

sideComponents.label: Symbol Table
sideComponents.description: Stores identifiers, types, scope, memory location — accessed by ALL phases

sideComponents.label: Error Handler
sideComponents.description: Reports and recovers from errors at every phase (lexical, syntax, semantic, etc.)

theory:
A compiler is a program that translates source code written in a high-level programming language into machine code or an intermediate representation. The compilation process is divided into 6 systematic phases, broadly grouped into the Front End (analysis of source program) and Back End (synthesis of target program).

Front End (Analysis Phase):

Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (Scanner): The lexer reads the source program character by character and groups characters into meaningful sequences called lexemes. Each lexeme is mapped to a token (e.g., keyword, identifier, operator, literal). Whitespace and comments are discarded. Tool: Lex/Flex.

Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parser): The parser takes the token stream and verifies the syntactic structure against the language grammar (expressed in BNF or EBNF). It builds an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) that represents the hierarchical structure of the program. Errors: syntax errors (missing semicolon, unmatched brackets). Tools: Yacc/Bison.

Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis: Checks for semantic correctness — type checking (cannot assign string to int), undeclared variables, scope rules, function signature matching. The AST is annotated with type information and the Symbol Table is updated.

Back End (Synthesis Phase):

Phase 4 — Intermediate Code Generation: Generates a machine-independent intermediate representation (IR), typically Three-Address Code (TAC) of the form a = b op c. This allows the back end to be retargeted to different architectures.

Phase 5 — Code Optimization: Improves the IR without changing program semantics. Techniques: constant folding (3+4 → 7), dead code elimination, loop invariant code motion, common subexpression elimination, inlining.

Phase 6 — Code Generation: Converts optimized IR to target machine code or assembly. Key tasks: register allocation (Belady's algorithm), instruction selection (choosing efficient opcodes), and instruction scheduling.

Symbol Table & Error Handler span all 6 phases as global data structures.
keyPoints: 6 phases: Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → IR Generation → Optimization → Code Generation
Front End (Analysis): Phases 1-3 deal with source language; Back End (Synthesis): Phases 4-6 deal with target machine
Symbol Table stores identifier info (name, type, scope, address) and is accessed by all phases
Intermediate Code (Three-Address Code) makes compiler machine-independent and retargetable
Lexical errors: illegal characters; Syntax errors: grammar violations; Semantic errors: type mismatches
useCases: GCC, Clang, LLVM — production compilers for C, C++, Rust using all 6 phases
JVM — Java compiler produces bytecode (IR) which JIT compiles to machine code at runtime
Database query compilers — SQL is parsed, semantically analyzed, optimized, and executed
